stories.headline: EQT acquires Coller Capital in landmark secondaries deal adding nearly $50bn AUM as PE firms target scale in private markets
stories.summary: **EQT**, the Swedish-listed private equity giant, has announced the acquisition of **Coller Capital**, the global secondaries specialist, in a deal that brings Coller's approximately **$50 billion of assets under management** onto EQT's platform. The transaction, reported in MarshBerry's January 2026 UK investment sector M&A roundup, is one of eight UK investment sector deals announced in January 2026 valued above £5 million, underscoring the strong opening to dealmaking activity this year. Coller Capital is one of the world's leading providers of liquidity solutions to both general partners and limited partners, investing across **private equity secondaries** and **private credit secondaries** — a market segment that has grown substantially as holding periods lengthen and sponsor-to-sponsor processes multiply. The acquisition directly accelerates EQT's stated ambition to develop a private markets firm of scale, adding Coller's 330 professionals across 11 global offices. No deal value has been publicly disclosed, and advisers on each side have not been named in the available sources. The strategic rationale is straightforward: secondaries dry powder has reached record levels globally, and embedding a dedicated secondaries capability gives EQT a structural advantage in GP-led continuation fund transactions — a format Shoosmiths' 2026 PE outlook identifies as expected to have a record-breaking year. PwC's UK M&A analysis confirms financial services delivered several of the UK's largest deals of 2025, with deal value in the sector rising **44% year-on-year**, and this transaction fits squarely within the asset and wealth management consolidation wave. The **Skadden** UK Public M&A outlook notes that both strategic and financial buyers remained active through 2025 despite macroeconomic uncertainty, and the EQT-Coller deal reflects the premium-asset focus that characterised 2025's two-speed market. Regulatory approvals will be required across multiple jurisdictions given EQT's global footprint, though no specific timeline has been disclosed. The deal is expected to close subject to customary conditions, with no break fee publicly reported.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Linklaters** and **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer** are natural candidates for this mandate — Linklaters has deep relationships with Nordic and Swedish PE houses including EQT, and its funds and asset management practice spans the GP-led secondaries structures at the heart of this deal. **Slaughter and May** has a strong track record advising on financial services M&A in the UK, which would be relevant to the target's UK-regulated entities. **Herbert Smith Freehills** is well-positioned given its funds practice and established work on cross-border private markets transactions. Any **FCA** authorisation or change-of-control consent for Coller's UK-regulated entities would require specialist UK regulatory counsel.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Kirkland & Ellis** dominates UK and European PE fund formation and fund-level M&A, and its London office regularly advises EQT on structuring work — this deal's PE-on-PE nature makes Kirkland the most likely US firm involved. **Latham & Watkins** has a strong secondaries and fund finance practice in London and would be competitive for the financing or fund-level structuring aspects of the transaction.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A first-seat trainee on this deal would be running **conditions precedent checklists** across multiple regulatory jurisdictions, coordinating FCA change-of-control notifications for Coller's UK-regulated entities, and assisting with due diligence on Coller's LP base and fund structures. The deal connects directly to the GP-led secondaries boom: Shoosmiths' 2026 outlook identifies continuation funds as expected to have a **record-breaking year**, meaning trainees entering PE or funds seats in 2026 will encounter this transaction type repeatedly.

stories.talkingPoint: EQT's acquisition of Coller Capital is the most significant signal yet that the largest PE houses are vertically integrating secondaries capabilities rather than remaining bystanders in the liquidity solutions market — a market Shoosmiths identifies as heading for a record year in 2026. For Magic Circle and US firms alike, the real opportunity is in the fund-level structuring and multi-jurisdictional regulatory clearance work that accompanies PE-on-PE deals of this complexity, where the adviser's fund finance and regulatory bench matters as much as their M&A capability.

stories.headline: Valterra Platinum completes £7.5bn London Stock Exchange listing following Anglo American demerger as UK equity capital markets reform beds in
stories.summary: **Valterra Platinum**, a South Africa-incorporated platinum group metals mining, production and marketing company, has completed a **£7.5 billion listing** on the **London Stock Exchange's Main Market** following its demerger from **Anglo American plc**, with **Macfarlanes** acting as adviser on the transaction. The deal represents one of the most significant primary market events on the LSE in recent memory and arrives as the UK's reformed listing regime — which came into force following the **FCA**'s overhaul of the Listing Rules — continues to bed in. Under the new regime, as noted in Lexology's UK Corporate Briefing for March 2026, issuers are required on an IPO to make a single application to list all securities of the relevant class, a structural change designed to simplify the admission process. Paul Hastings' UK Equity Capital Markets Insights for March 2026 highlights several contemporaneous regulatory and governance developments shaping the environment: on **24 February 2026**, the **FTSE Women Leaders Review** published its final annual review, and on **26 February 2026**, the **City of London Law Society** published revised specimen documents relating to Admission Conditions and Further Terms of the Offer for Takeover Code purposes. The Bank of England's January 2026 capital issuance statistics confirm active bond and equity issuance activity by UK residents at the start of the year, providing the macro backdrop. The Valterra listing is strategically significant because it tests London's attractiveness as a venue for large natural resources listings at a time when the LSE has faced competition from New York and Johannesburg for mining sector capital. Anglo American's decision to demerger the platinum assets and list them in London rather than pursue a dual-listing or US float is a meaningful vote of confidence in the reformed UK regime. No specific debt structure or bookrunner syndicate is named in the available sources. The transaction is complete, with no outstanding regulatory conditions reported.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Macfarlanes** has the direct mandate on this transaction and its capital markets practice is confirmed as advising Valterra Platinum. **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer** and **Linklaters** are the dominant Magic Circle firms for FTSE 100-level equity capital markets work and would be natural counterparties or underwriter counsel on a transaction of this scale. The reformed **FCA** Listing Rules and the **Takeover Code** changes documented by the CLLS on 26 February 2026 mean that UK-qualified equity capital markets counsel is essential, giving London-headquartered firms a structural advantage over US rivals on admission documentation.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Sullivan & Cromwell** and **Davis Polk** have strong equity capital markets practices for cross-border mining and natural resources issuers and could act for underwriters or institutional investors with US-law governed participation agreements. However, the primary listing work and UKLA-facing documentation is firmly in the domain of UK-qualified firms.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in the capital markets seat on this deal would be assisting with the single-application listing documentation required under the new **FCA** regime, running verification of the prospectus, and tracking the CLLS revised specimen documents published on **26 February 2026** to ensure the Conditions and Further Terms are compliant with the updated Takeover Code templates. The Valterra listing connects to the broader trend of London fighting to retain large natural resources and mining listings, a structural challenge that the FCA's listing reform was explicitly designed to address.

stories.talkingPoint: Valterra Platinum's **£7.5 billion** LSE listing is exactly the kind of flagship transaction the FCA's reformed Listing Rules were designed to attract — a large, internationally significant issuer choosing London over alternative venues. The fact that Macfarlanes, rather than a Magic Circle firm, is leading the issuer-side mandate is a reminder that the Silver Circle is highly competitive for major equity capital markets mandates where deep partner relationships with extractive sector clients matter more than firm size.

stories.headline: Private credit market pushes back against Blue Owl's expansion caution as MUFG hires from Crédit Agricole to build out London leveraged finance bench
stories.summary: The private credit market is experiencing a notable moment of internal tension, with GlobalCapital reporting on **3 March 2026** that **private credit players are pushing back** after **Blue Owl Capital** cast doubt on the sector's near-term expansion prospects. The dispute reflects a broader strategic divergence among direct lenders about whether the asset class has reached a point of structural saturation or remains in a growth phase, particularly as broadly syndicated loan markets have reopened and are competing more aggressively for sponsor-backed transactions. Simultaneously, **MUFG** has hired from **Crédit Agricole** to strengthen its leveraged finance team, a personnel move reported by GlobalCapital on the same date that signals continued institutional bank investment in the leveraged finance origination capability even as private credit competes for the same paper. The DLA Piper Private Credit Academy — scheduled for delivery with speakers including Partner **Alan Rockwell** and Partner **Maria Cornilsen** — has explicitly framed its curriculum around comparing **private credit and syndicated loan deal terms**, confirming that the practical convergence and divergence of these two markets is a live issue for practitioners. Fitch Ratings' 2026 Leveraged Finance Credit Outlook event features **Jonathan Bock** of **Blackstone Private Credit** and **Lyuba Petrova**, Fitch's Head of US Leveraged Finance, as keynote participants, with Fitch identifying credit quality and spread compression as the key risk themes for the year. The Citizens Financial survey cited in Private Equity Wire found that **58% of middle-market executives and PE professionals** expect M&A volumes to increase in 2026 after a 2025 dominated by large-cap transactions — rising deal flow directly drives leveraged finance origination. The tension between private credit's flexibility and the syndicated market's improving liquidity terms is the defining structural question for leveraged finance practitioners in 2026. No specific deal values or financing structures are named in the available sources beyond the Blue Owl commentary. The MUFG hire is an undisclosed compensation arrangement.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Clifford Chance** and **Allen & Overy Shearman** have the deepest leveraged finance practices among Magic Circle firms in London and act regularly for both syndicated lenders and private credit funds on sponsor-backed transactions. **Ashurst** and **Hogan Lovells** are well-positioned in the Silver Circle for mid-market direct lending documentation work. The convergence of syndicated and private credit terms — the explicit focus of DLA Piper's training programme — means that UK firms with strong **LMA**-standard documentation expertise hold a durable advantage as deal terms evolve.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Kirkland & Ellis** and **Latham & Watkins** dominate the leveraged finance documentation market in London for US-sponsor backed transactions, with Latham in particular having a bench specifically noted for its depth in **broadly syndicated** and **direct lending** deal comparison work. The MUFG hire from Crédit Agricole suggests institutional banks are investing in origination to compete with private credit funds for **Kirkland** and **Latham**-advised mandates.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee or NQ in the banking and finance seat would be reviewing and red-lining **LMA leveraged finance facility agreements**, comparing covenant packages between syndicated and private credit term sheets, and tracking the **Blue Owl** market commentary as context for client advice on refinancing optionality. The Citizens Financial finding that **58%** of middle-market professionals expect increased M&A volumes in 2026 is the direct pipeline signal that leveraged finance desks are positioning around.

stories.talkingPoint: The Blue Owl pushback story is more than an inter-firm spat — it crystallises the structural question of whether private credit's spread advantage is durable as broadly syndicated markets recover liquidity and institutional banks like **MUFG** invest in their leveraged finance origination capability. For trainees targeting banking and finance seats, understanding the precise differences between LMA syndicated loan terms and private credit documentation is becoming a baseline competency, not an advanced specialism.

stories.headline: Trump secures voluntary pledge from major tech companies to self-fund AI data centre power costs as White House frames deal as consumer electricity price protection
stories.summary: On **4 March 2026**, the **White House** announced that major technology companies had signed a voluntary pledge committing to cover the costs of powering their **AI data centres** themselves, with President Trump framing the agreement as a mechanism to prevent rising AI energy demand from passing through to consumer electricity bills. The pledge, reported across the New York Times, Los Angeles Times, and Associated Press, does not name specific financial commitments or deal values per company, and the White House offered limited structural detail about enforcement or verification mechanisms. The signatories include companies at the centre of the accelerating **AI infrastructure build-out** — the same investment wave that PwC's UK M&A analysis identifies as a driver of rising UK deal values in 2025 through **large-scale infrastructure demand**. The Trump administration's approach is explicitly voluntary rather than regulatory, contrasting with the European Union's binding framework under the **EU AI Act** and the **Energy Efficiency Directive** as applied to data centres. The strategic tension is commercially significant: large technology companies are simultaneously the largest consumers of new power infrastructure and the parties most exposed to regulatory risk if that consumption is seen to raise household energy costs. For energy lawyers, the pledge creates a transactional impetus — companies that have committed to self-funding power will need to execute **power purchase agreements**, **grid connection contracts**, **on-site generation structures**, and potentially **battery storage arrangements** at scale and speed. The Associated Press noted that Trump claimed the deal would lower electricity prices, but provided no mechanism or modelling to support that assertion, leaving the legal and regulatory architecture of the pledge largely undefined. No specific law firm advisers are named in the available sources.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Clifford Chance** and **Linklaters** have dominant energy and infrastructure practices advising on large-scale **power purchase agreements** and grid connection work across the US and Europe — the precise transaction types that tech companies will need to execute at scale to honour the White House pledge. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has a well-regarded energy regulatory practice and is active on data centre energy infrastructure transactions. UK firms with US offices are particularly well-placed as the transactional work will be governed by US law but structured by advisers with global energy infrastructure capability.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Latham & Watkins** has the deepest bench in the US for energy project finance and power purchase agreements, and is the natural choice for technology companies executing large-scale energy procurement structures. **Kirkland & Ellis** is competitive for the PE-backed energy infrastructure and data centre development transactions that the pledge will catalyse.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in the energy or projects seat would be drafting **power purchase agreement** heads of terms, reviewing grid connection offer letters from US transmission operators, and tracking the regulatory framework — including any Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (**FERC**) filings — triggered by data centre self-supply commitments. The White House pledge connects directly to the structural trend of **AI-driven infrastructure demand** that PwC identifies as one of the two primary drivers lifting UK and global deal values in 2025 and into 2026.

stories.talkingPoint: Trump's voluntary data centre power pledge is legally thin — no enforcement mechanism, no defined financial commitment, no regulatory backstop — but its transactional consequences are substantial: every signatory now has a political commitment to execute energy self-supply structures, creating a wave of **PPAs**, grid contracts, and on-site generation deals that will keep energy project finance practices at full capacity through 2026 and beyond. The contrast with the **EU AI Act**'s binding energy efficiency requirements for data centres illustrates exactly why transatlantic regulatory divergence is the defining structural challenge for tech companies with operations in both jurisdictions.

stories.headline: European Commission closes medical device dominance probe and opens new Foreign Subsidies Regulation investigation into Chinese wind turbine maker Goldwind as Brussels sharpens its enforcement toolkit
stories.summary: On **16 February 2026**, the **European Commission** closed an investigation into whether a medical device manufacturer's policy restricting physicians from cooperating with a competitor constituted an **abuse of dominant position** under **EU competition law**, as reported in McGuireWoods' European Competition Law Newsletter for March 2026. The closure without a finding of infringement follows a broader pattern the **BCLP** and **Macfarlanes** competition teams have identified: both the **CMA** and the Commission are under pressure to prioritise enforcement resources on cases with the greatest economic impact. In parallel, the Commission has opened a new investigation under the **Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR)** into **Goldwind**, a Chinese wind turbine manufacturer active in the EU through its subsidiary **Vensys**, marking the second FSR probe focused on a Chinese company. The Goldwind investigation was initiated in **April 2024** via requests for information to companies active in the EU wind sector, and the Commission's decision to escalate to a formal investigation signals that the FSR is becoming an active enforcement mechanism rather than a dormant deterrent. Under the **FSR**, the Commission has power to investigate financial contributions granted by non-EU governments — principally China — to companies operating in the EU, and can impose remedies including divestiture or financial redress. The Steptoe regulatory landscape paper for 2026 confirms that the **CMA** underwent a decisive reset in 2025 — including the sacking of its Chair in January 2025, acceptance of remedies in the **Vodafone/Three** 4:3 telecoms merger in March, and closure of the three-year cloud services investigation in July — and is now prioritising transactions with substantive UK competitive impact. No fines have been announced in the Goldwind investigation. The FSR probe runs concurrently with the Commission's existing anti-dumping and anti-subsidy investigations into Chinese wind turbine imports.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer** and **Linklaters** have the strongest Brussels-based EU competition practices and are the natural choices for FSR advisory work given the regulation's novelty and its intersection with foreign direct investment screening. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has a well-regarded EU competition and state aid practice and has been active on FSR compliance. The **CMA**'s concurrent reset creates a domestic parallel — UK firms with strong CMA relationships benefit from advising on the new, more targeted merger control approach.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** has a dominant Brussels competition practice and is consistently mandated on novel EU enforcement initiatives, making it the leading US firm for FSR defence work. **Skadden** has a strong EU competition presence and its January 2026 UK Public M&A outlook demonstrates its engagement with the regulatory landscape that intersects with competition clearance.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in the competition seat would be preparing **FSR notification documents**, reviewing information requests from the Commission under **Article 14 FSR**, and tracking the parallel anti-subsidy proceedings to map overlapping exposure for clients with Chinese supply chains. The Goldwind investigation connects to the macro trend of **EU economic security regulation** — the FSR, alongside the Foreign Direct Investment Screening Regulation, represents a structural shift in how Brussels treats non-EU state-backed capital.

stories.talkingPoint: The **Goldwind FSR investigation** is a signal that the Foreign Subsidies Regulation has moved from a paper tiger into an active enforcement tool — the Commission is now using it against a major Chinese industrial actor in a strategically sensitive sector, not merely running procedural test cases. For law firms, the FSR creates a durable new practice area sitting at the intersection of EU competition law, state aid, and geopolitical risk management, and the firms that built FSR teams early — notably **Freshfields**, **Linklaters**, and **Cleary Gottlieb** — are well-positioned to capture the coming wave of advisory mandates.

stories.headline: UK High Court finds BHP liable under Brazilian law for 2015 Fundão tailings dam collapse in landmark environmental mass tort ruling with major implications for cross-border group litigation
stories.summary: The **UK High Court** has found **BHP** liable under **Brazilian law** for the **2015 collapse of the Fundão tailings dam** in Brazil, described by Morrison Foerster's UK Litigation 2025 Recap and 2026 Outlook as the worst environmental disaster in Brazil's history, causing extensive environmental damage and loss of life. The case — **Mariana v BHP Group [2025] EWHC 3001 (TCC)** — is a landmark ruling because it confirms that the English courts are willing to apply foreign law to hold a UK-domiciled parent company liable for the conduct of overseas subsidiaries in respect of catastrophic environmental events. The judgment has immediate implications for the trajectory of **group litigation** in England and Wales, which TLT's Disputes Outlook 2026 identifies as one of the most significant structural trends shaping commercial disputes this year, driven by claimant law firms, litigation funders, and evolving court strategies. The Fundão dam collapse released approximately 60 million cubic metres of mining waste, destroyed the town of Bento Rodrigues, and contaminated the Rio Doce river for hundreds of kilometres. The quantum phase of the litigation — determining the precise financial liability — remains to be resolved, and the damages exposure for BHP could be substantial given the scale of the environmental harm and the number of affected individuals and communities. The Morrison Foerster outlook also notes the contemporaneous **Supreme Court ruling in King v Crude** on the Mackay v Dick doctrine of deemed fulfilment, a distinct but connected development confirming the Supreme Court's appetite for resolving foundational contract law questions. No specific adviser names are identified in the sources for the BHP litigation. The ruling will be studied closely by mining, energy, and infrastructure companies with UK parent entities and overseas operational subsidiaries.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Herbert Smith Freehills** has one of the UK's strongest international arbitration and complex litigation practices with deep mining sector expertise, making it the leading firm for both defendant-side and claimant-side instructions on cases of this type. **Freshfields Bruckhaus Deringer** and **Clifford Chance** are active in large-scale environmental and group litigation defence. The **English courts**' willingness to apply Brazilian law and exercise jurisdiction over a UK-listed parent gives London firms a durable competitive advantage as the forum of choice for cross-border mass environmental tort claims.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** and **Sullivan & Cromwell** have strong cross-border litigation capabilities and act for major mining and extractive sector clients in complex multi-jurisdictional disputes. The BHP liability finding may generate follow-on work in the US for any parallel proceedings or investor claims.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in the disputes seat following this case would be tracking the **quantum trial** timetable, reviewing the application of **Brazilian environmental law** as applied by the TCC, and preparing case summaries on the group litigation procedure for partner briefings. The Fundão judgment connects directly to the broader trend identified by TLT: **group proceedings** underpinned by litigation funding are now a structural feature of the English commercial courts, not an exceptional event.

stories.talkingPoint: The **Mariana v BHP** judgment is a watershed for cross-border environmental mass tort litigation — it confirms that the English High Court will pierce the corporate veil of geography and apply foreign law to hold UK-listed parents accountable for overseas subsidiary conduct at scale. For commercial lawyers, the practical consequence is that every UK-headquartered multinational with overseas operational exposure needs to audit its environmental liability profile now, before the quantum phase of cases like this one sets a damages template that claimant funders will replicate systematically.

stories.headline: European Commission probes Goldwind under Foreign Subsidies Regulation in second China-focused investigation as EU tightens scrutiny of state-backed capital in strategic sectors
stories.summary: The **European Commission** has opened a formal investigation under the **Foreign Subsidies Regulation (FSR)** into **Goldwind**, a Chinese company active in the production and sale of **wind turbines** and related services in the EU through its subsidiary **Vensys**, as reported in McGuireWoods' March 2026 European Competition Law Newsletter. This is the second FSR investigation focused on a Chinese entity, reinforcing the pattern that the Commission views the FSR primarily as a tool to address **Chinese state subsidies** flowing into strategically sensitive EU sectors. The probe began in **April 2024** with requests for information to companies active in the EU wind sector, and the Commission's escalation to a formal investigation in early 2026 marks a significant procedural step. The **FSR**, which came into force in July 2023, empowers the Commission to investigate and redress **foreign financial contributions** — defined broadly to include grants, loans, guarantees, and other state support — granted to companies operating in the EU by non-EU governments. Remedies available under the FSR include behavioural commitments, structural measures, and financial contributions to level the competitive playing field. The Goldwind investigation runs alongside existing **EU anti-dumping** and **anti-subsidy measures** targeting Chinese wind turbine manufacturers, creating a layered regulatory exposure for the company and its EU counterparties. Mayer Brown's Europe Daily News of **4 March 2026** notes that the **Trade Policy Committee (Deputies)** agenda for 5 March 2026 includes items under **EU anti-dumping Regulation (EU) 2016/1036**, reflecting the active use of the EU's trade defence toolkit in parallel with the FSR. The investigation has no disclosed timeline for completion, and no fines or remedies have yet been imposed. The practical consequence for EU wind energy procurement — including from UK-based energy companies sourcing Chinese turbines — is heightened legal uncertainty around supply chain compliance.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Linklaters** and **Freshfields** are the dominant UK firms for EU trade law, state aid, and FSR advisory work, with established Brussels offices and dedicated foreign subsidies teams built ahead of the FSR's entry into force. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has an active trade and foreign investment practice and advises on overlapping **NSIB** and foreign investment screening matters in the UK, which often arise alongside FSR issues for dual-listed or UK-nexus companies. The FSR's interaction with UK procurement rules post-Brexit means UK firms advising on infrastructure and energy procurement also need to track Commission FSR findings.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Cleary Gottlieb** Brussels is the leading US firm on novel EU trade and competition enforcement, and has been at the forefront of FSR advisory work since the regulation's inception. **Skadden** and **Davis Polk** also have active EU trade practices and would be competitive for FSR defence mandates for US or globally-listed companies with Chinese supply chain exposure.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee in the EU trade or competition seat would be reviewing **FSR notification thresholds** and preparing compliance matrices for clients tendering in EU public procurement, tracking the Commission's information requests in the Goldwind investigation, and cross-referencing with **anti-dumping Regulation (EU) 2016/1036** filings. The investigation connects to the structural macro trend of **EU economic security** legislation — the FSR, FDI Screening Regulation, and Critical Raw Materials Act are together reshaping how non-EU state capital is treated across the single market.

stories.talkingPoint: The **Goldwind FSR investigation** confirms that Brussels has selected Chinese wind turbines as a test case for aggressive **Foreign Subsidies Regulation** enforcement in a strategically critical clean energy sector — and the choice is deliberate, given the EU's simultaneous push to expand domestic wind capacity under the **Green Deal Industrial Plan**. For international trade lawyers in London, the FSR is now an unavoidable element of any cross-border transaction or procurement involving non-EU state-backed entities, and firms that built FSR capability early are seeing that investment pay dividends in a way that will only intensify as the Commission expands its enforcement pipeline.

stories.headline: US Department of Justice establishes AI Litigation Task Force and FTC faces March 11 deadline for Section 5 policy statement as federal AI regulatory architecture takes shape under Trump Executive Order
stories.summary: **Attorney General Pam Bondi** established the **Department of Justice's AI Litigation Task Force** on **9 January 2026**, tasked with challenging **state AI laws** in federal court on grounds including unconstitutional burdens on interstate commerce, preemption by existing federal regulations, and any other basis the Attorney General deems appropriate, as reported by Baker Botts. The Task Force has not yet filed any lawsuits, in part because the Executive Order it operates under contemplates a sequenced process: the **Department of Commerce** must first identify specific state laws for referral before DOJ can act. The second major federal AI regulatory milestone is a policy statement from the **Federal Trade Commission** due by **11 March 2026**, setting out the Commission's position on the application of **Section 5 of the FTC Act** — prohibiting unfair and deceptive acts or practices — to **AI models**. These two developments together define the emerging federal AI regulatory framework under the Trump administration: aggressive pre-emption of state-level regulation, combined with a narrowly framed federal consumer protection overlay through the FTC. The National Law Review's analysis of new state AI laws identifies **California** as the most active jurisdiction, with pending bills requiring covered providers of **AI-generated content** with over 1 million monthly visitors to provide clear disclosures, offer users opt-in AI labelling tools, and provide AI detection capabilities. The Jenner & Block article in The Times, published **5 March 2026**, identifies concerns about **AI use in official governmental decisions** and compliance questions under existing administrative law frameworks. For law firms, the Baker Botts analysis makes clear that the federal-state tension in AI regulation creates immediate compliance obligations: firms advising technology clients must now map their clients' AI deployments against both the emerging federal framework and the patchwork of state laws that the DOJ Task Force has not yet challenged but may do so imminently. The **EU AI Act** implementation timeline runs in parallel, creating a bifurcated compliance environment for firms with transatlantic operations.

whyItMatters.ukFirms: **Clifford Chance** and **Linklaters** have built dedicated AI regulatory practices spanning both the **EU AI Act** and US state law compliance, and are the most naturally positioned Magic Circle firms to advise multinational clients navigating the transatlantic regulatory divergence. **Herbert Smith Freehills** has an active technology regulatory practice and has published extensively on **EU AI Act** implementation. The **FTC Section 5** policy statement due **11 March 2026** will be required reading for UK firms advising US-market technology clients.
whyItMatters.usFirms: **Sidley Austin** has a dedicated AI law practice explicitly focused on both AI developers and AI deployers across financial services, healthcare, and technology, and is well-positioned to advise on the DOJ Task Force's pre-emption strategy and FTC policy statement. **Latham & Watkins** and **Davis Polk** are competitive for large technology company AI compliance mandates given their existing client relationships in the sector.
whyItMatters.onTheGround: A trainee or NQ in the technology regulatory or corporate seat would be preparing **state AI law compliance matrices** for clients operating across multiple US jurisdictions, tracking the **Department of Commerce** referral process that triggers DOJ enforcement, and monitoring the **FTC Section 5 policy statement** due **11 March 2026** for guidance on AI model marketing and deployment disclosures. The DOJ Task Force connects to a structural trend identified by the National Law Review: the number of active state AI laws is accelerating faster than federal pre-emption capacity, creating a compliance window that clients urgently need to manage.

stories.talkingPoint: The **DOJ AI Litigation Task Force** and the **FTC Section 5** deadline on **11 March 2026** together represent the clearest signal yet that the Trump administration is serious about federalising AI regulation — not through new legislation, but through aggressive pre-emption of state laws and the use of existing consumer protection frameworks. For law firms, the practical implication is that AI compliance advice can no longer be delivered jurisdiction by jurisdiction: clients need a unified federal-state-EU framework analysis, and the firms building that capacity now will own the most commercially valuable advisory relationships in the technology sector through the rest of this decade.

sectorWatch.body: The **European Commission**'s escalation of its **Foreign Subsidies Regulation** investigation into **Goldwind** — the second China-focused FSR probe in two years — marks the moment the regulation transitions from a compliance framework into an active enforcement tool. Running alongside **EU anti-dumping measures** under Regulation 2016/1036 and the FDI Screening Regulation, the FSR is creating a layered legal exposure for any EU market participant sourcing from or partnering with state-backed non-EU entities. **Freshfields**, **Linklaters**, and **Cleary Gottlieb** Brussels are the firms that built FSR practices earliest and are best positioned to capture the growing advisory pipeline. Law students targeting international trade and competition seats should treat FSR fluency as a baseline requirement for 2026 and beyond.

oneToFollow.story: FTC Section 5 policy statement on AI models due 11 March 2026 — the first federal guidance on how consumer protection law applies to AI deployment, marketing, and outputs
oneToFollow.why: The statement will define the outer boundaries of permissible AI model conduct under existing US federal law and will be immediately relevant to every technology company advising on AI product deployment, advertising, and disclosure — making it the most practically significant single regulatory document in the AI compliance calendar this month. Technology regulatory, corporate, and data privacy practice groups at both Magic Circle and US firms will need to integrate the FTC's position into client advice across financial services, healthcare, and consumer technology sectors within days of publication.
